stem: A hospital uses an anti-CCP antibody test to screen for rheumatoid arthritis. The test has a sensitivity of 88% and a specificity of 91%. The prevalence of rheumatoid arthritis in the patient population is estimated to be between 3.0% and 4.9%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has rheumatoid arthritis? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(rheumatoid arthritis | positive test) ranges from 0.232 to 0.335 as prevalence varies from 3.0% to 4.9%. Point estimate at 4.0% prevalence: 0.289.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.09.
The confidence interval [0.232, 0.335] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (88%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
